summary: claw-subagent-service silently registers a Windows service that exfiltrates LLM session data and accepts remote control commands from the publisher's IM channel
details:
On Windows, when `npm install -g claw-subagent-service` is run from an elevated shell, scripts/post-install.js detects admin via `net session` and silently calls `svc.install()` + `svc.start()` (node-windows) to register and auto-start a persistent service named `claw-subagent-service`, configured via `sc.exe` for auto-start and automatic restart on failure. No prompt, no opt-out, no README acknowledgement gate.

Once running, the service connects to the publisher's RongCloud IM cluster (appKey `bmdehs6pbyyks`, server `https://newsradar.dreamdt.cn/im`, hardcoded in service/worker.js) and does two things:

1. Silent relay of installer data: service/modules/heartbeat-dashboard.js sets a 30-second interval that reads `~/.openclaw/agents/*/sessions/*.jsonl` (LLM session transcripts, prompts, model usage, costs) and ships the data as DASHBOARD_USAGE_EVENTS messages over the publisher's IM channel. The installer never opts in; the channel destination is fixed to the publisher.

2. Remote command backdoor: service/modules/rongyun-message-handler.js handles COMMAND, DEVICE_CONTROL, CHAT_MESSAGE, and CREATE/DELETE_OPENCODE_SESSION messages from the same channel. DEVICE_CONTROL `delete` calls `ServiceManager.stop()` + `ServiceManager.uninstall()` and `fs.unlinkSync` on config files; COMMAND executes shipped scripts under command/win|linux/ and runs `openclaw doctor --fix` via `executeCommandDirect`. Anyone with publish access to the author's RongCloud account can stop, uninstall, or run shipped commands on every installed host.

Amplifying the surface, service/updater.js polls `npm view claw-subagent-service version` every 6 hours on Windows and, when a newer version is published, runs `npm install -g claw-subagent-service@<version>` and restarts the worker without operator approval — any future version (or a maintainer-credential compromise) is auto-deployed to every installed Windows host.

The combination of silent admin-time service registration, a publisher-controlled remote-command channel, exfiltration of local LLM session content, and unattended auto-update meets the bar for a supply-chain backdoor.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
